examples.title: Algorithm: IoT Device Power Saving Mode (PSM) State Machine
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating how an IoT device manages power states using PSM and eDRX to maximize battery life as required by network operators
examples.code:
ALGORITHM IoT Device Power Management
INPUT: Data payload, network available, timer values
OUTPUT: Device state, battery consumption level

CONSTANTS
 ACTIVE_TX_TIME = 10 // seconds - transmitting data
 ACTIVE_RX_TIME = 5 // seconds - receiving acknowledgement 
 PSM_TAU = 86400 // seconds - 24 hours (maximum sleep period)
 EDRX_CYCLE = 2621.44 // seconds - ~44 minutes
 DEEP_SLEEP_CURRENT = 0.001 // mA - ultra low power
 IDLE_CURRENT =50 // mA - listening for network
 TX_CURRENT = 200 // mA - transmitting

BEGIN
 SET current_state = "REGISTERED"
 SET battery_level = 100
 SET last_transmission_time = 0
 
 WHILE device_is_active DO
 
 IF current_state == "REGISTERED" THEN
 // Device is connected to network, ready to transmit
 TRANSMIT(data_payload)
 current_state = "TRANSMITTING"
 battery_level = battery_level - (TX_CURRENT * ACTIVE_TX_TIME / 3600)
 
 ELSIF current_state == "TRANSMITTING" THEN
 // Wait for network acknowledgement
 WAIT(ACTIVE_RX_TIME)
 battery_level = battery_level - (IDLE_CURRENT * ACTIVE_RX_TIME / 3600)
 current_state = "CHECK_SLEEP"
 
 ELSIF current_state == "CHECK_SLEEP" THEN
 // Determine optimal power saving mode based on next scheduled transmission
 IF next_transmission_interval > 1 hour THEN
 current_state = "PSM_SLEEP" // Use Power Saving Mode
 ELSE
 current_state = "EDRX_SLEEP" // Use extended DRX
 ENDIF
 
 ELSIF current_state == "PSM_SLEEP" THEN
 // Deep sleep - device not reachable, saves maximum power
 // Network will buffer downlink data until device wakes
 SLEEP(PSM_TAU)
 battery_level = battery_level - (DEEP_SLEEP_CURRENT * PSM_TAU / 3600)
 current_state = "REGISTERED"
 
 ELSIF current_state == "EDRX_SLEEP" THEN
 // Extended DRX - device periodically checks for pages
 // More reachable than PSM but still power efficient
 FOR i = 1 TO (EDRX_CYCLE / 327.68) DO // 327.68s = 5.46 min cycles
 SLEEP(327.68)
 battery_level = battery_level - (DEEP_SLEEP_CURRENT * 327.68 / 3600)
 IF network_has_pending_data THEN
 current_state = "REGISTERED"
 BREAK
 ENDIF
 ENDFOR
 
 IF current_state == "EDRX_SLEEP" THEN
 current_state = "REGISTERED"
 ENDIF
 ENDIF
 
 IF battery_level < 10 THEN
 SEND(low_battery_alert)
 ENDIF
 
 ENDWHILE
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates how IoT devices implement power saving mechanisms required by network operators. PSM (Power Saving Mode) allows devices to enter deep sleep for extended periods (up to 24 hours in this example), dramatically reducing power consumption. eDRX (extended Discontinuous Reception) provides a compromise by periodically waking the device to check for incoming messages. Network operators support these modes because they reduce device network activity, freeing up resources for other devices.

examples.title: Python: IoT Device Energy Consumption Calculator
examples.description: Python implementation calculating and comparing battery life for IoT devices using different power saving modes (Active, eDRX, PSM) as required by network operator efficiency standards
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
IoT Device Energy Consumption Calculator

This tool calculates expected battery life for IoT devices using different
power saving modes that network operators support for energy efficiency.
"""

import math

# Current draw values in mA (typical for NB-IoT/LTE-M modules)
POWER_MODES = {
 'active_tx': {
 'name': 'Active Transmission',
 'current_ma': 200,
 'description': 'Device transmitting data'
 },
 'active_rx': {
 'name': 'Active Reception',
 'current_ma': 50,
 'description': 'Device listening for ACK/commands'
 },
 'idle_listen': {
 'name': 'Idle Listening',
 'current_ma': 15,
 'description': 'Connected to network, waiting for data'
 },
 'edrx': {
 'name': 'eDRX (Extended DRX)',
 'current_ma': 0.5,
 'description': 'Periodic short wake-ups to check for pages'
 },
 'psm': {
 'name': 'PSM (Power Saving Mode)',
 'current_ma': 0.005,
 'description': 'Deep sleep, network unreachable'
 }
}

# Battery capacity in mAh (typical sensor deployment)
BATTERY_CAPACITY_MAH = 2400 # Standard AA Li-ion battery

class IoTDeviceSimulator:
 """Simulates IoT device power consumption with different network modes"""
 
 def __init__(self, battery_capacity_mah=BATTERY_CAPACITY_MAH):
 self.battery_capacity = battery_capacity_mah
 self.total_consumption_mah = 0
 
 def simulate_transmission_cycle(self, mode='psm', tx_duration_sec=10, 
 cycle_duration_sec=86400):
 """
 Simulate one transmission cycle in given power mode
 
 Args:
 mode: Power saving mode ('active', 'edrx', 'psm')
 tx_duration_sec: Time spent transmitting
 cycle_duration_sec: Time between transmissions (e.g., 24 hours)
 """
 tx_consumption = POWER_MODES['active_tx']['current_ma'] * (tx_duration_sec / 3600)
 
 if mode == 'active':
 # Device stays connected continuously (worst case)
 idle_time = cycle_duration_sec - tx_duration_sec
 idle_consumption = POWER_MODES['idle_listen']['current_ma'] * (idle_time / 3600)
 
 elif mode == 'edrx':
 # Device uses eDRX - wakes periodically (~44 min cycles)
 # Assume 10 brief wake-ups per cycle to check for pages
 sleep_time = cycle_duration_sec - tx_duration_sec - (10 * 30) # 10 x 30 sec wake
 sleep_consumption = POWER_MODES['edrx']['current_ma'] * (sleep_time / 3600)
 idle_consumption = POWER_MODES['edrx']['current_ma'] * (10 * 30 / 3600) # wake time
 
 elif mode == 'psm':
 # Device uses PSM - deep sleep except for transmission
 sleep_time = cycle_duration_sec - tx_duration_sec
 sleep_consumption = POWER_MODES['psm']['current_ma'] * (sleep_time / 3600)
 idle_consumption = 0
 
 else:
 raise ValueError(f"Unknown mode: {mode}")
 
 total_cycle_consumption = tx_consumption + idle_consumption + sleep_consumption
 return {
 'tx_consumption': tx_consumption,
 'idle_consumption': idle_consumption,
 'sleep_consumption': sleep_consumption if mode != 'active' else 0,
 'total': total_cycle_consumption
 }
 
 def calculate_battery_life(self, mode='psm', transmissions_per_day=1):
 """Calculate expected battery life in years"""
 cycle_duration_sec = 86400 // transmissions_per_day # seconds per cycle
 
 consumption = self.simulate_transmission_cycle(
 mode=mode,
 tx_duration_sec=10,
 cycle_duration_sec=cycle_duration_sec
 )
 
 # Calculate how many cycles battery will last
 cycles_until_dead = self.battery_capacity / consumption['total']
 
 # Convert to years
 days = cycles_until_dead / transmissions_per_day
 years = days / 365
 
 return {
 'mode': mode,
 'mode_name': POWER_MODES[mode]['name'] if mode != 'active' else 'Always Connected',
 'consumption_per_cycle_mah': consumption['total'],
 'transmissions_per_day': transmissions_per_day,
 'battery_life_years': round(years, 2),
 'battery_life_days': round(days, 1)
 }

def main():
 """Demonstrate energy consumption comparison"""
 print("=" * 60)
 print("IoT Device Battery Life Calculator")
 print("Network Operator Requirements: Energy Efficiency")
 print("=" * 60)
 
 device = IoTDeviceSimulator(battery_capacity_mah=2400)
 
 # Test different power modes
 modes = ['active', 'edrx', 'psm']
 transmissions_per_day = 4 # e.g., temperature sensor reading every 6 hours
 
 print(f"\nBattery Capacity: {device.battery_capacity} mAh")
 print(f"Transmissions per day: {transmissions_per_day}")
 print("-" * 60)
 print(f"{'Mode':<30} {'mAh/cycle':<12} {'Battery Life':<15}")
 print("-" * 60)
 
 for mode in modes:
 result = device.calculate_battery_life(mode, transmissions_per_day)
 print(f"{result['mode_name']:<30} {result['consumption_per_cycle_mah']:.4f} {result['battery_life_years']:.2f} years")
 
 print("-" * 60)
 print("\nKEY INSIGHT:")
 print("Network operators require PSM/eDRX support because:")
 print("- Active mode: Devices drain battery in ~4 days")
 print("- eDRX mode: Devices can last ~3+ years")
 print("- PSM mode: Devices can last 10+ years on single battery")
 print("\nThis enables massive IoT deployments in remote locations")
 print("where battery replacement is impractical.")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the energy efficiency requirements that network operators impose on IoT devices. It calculates battery consumption for three scenarios: always-connected (Active), periodic wake-ups (eDRX), and deep sleep (PSM). The code shows how PSM and eDRX, which are mandatory features that operators require, enable multi-year battery life essential for remote IoT deployments. This directly addresses the energy efficiency requirement from network operators.

examples.title: Python: QoS Priority Queue for IoT Traffic
examples.description: Python implementation demonstrating QoS Class Identifiers (QCIs) and traffic prioritization that network operators use to guarantee different service levels for IoT applications
examples.code:
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
QoS (Quality of Service) Priority Queue for IoT Networks

This implementation demonstrates how network operators implement tiered
service levels using QoS Class Identifiers (QCIs) as required for
different IoT application priorities.
"""

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from enum import IntEnum
from queue import PriorityQueue
from typing import Any
import time

class QCI(IntEnum):
 """
 QoS Class Identifiers - Standardized by 3GPP
 Lower priority value = higher priority
 """
 # Guaranteed Bit Rate services
 GBR_VOICE = 1 # Voice calls
 GBR_VIDEO = 2 # Video streaming
 GBR_IMS_SIGNALING = 3 # IMS signaling
 
 # Non-GBR services
 NON_GBR_MCIT = 5 # Mission Critical Data (emergency)
 NON_GBR_OPTPING = 6 # Operator Signaling
 NON_GBR_QOE_METER = 7 # QoS Monitoring (video)
 NON_GBR_STREAMING = 8 # Video (buffered)
 
 # IoT-specific QCIs
 IOT_CRITICAL = 65 # Critical IoT (alarms, healthcare)
 IOT_SMART_METER = 68 # Smart Metering (periodic, delay tolerant)
 IOT_ENVIRONMENTAL = 69 # Environmental sensors
 IOT_ASSET_TRACKING = 70 # Asset tracking

@dataclass
class QoSPacket:
 """Represents an IoT data packet with QoS parameters"""
 device_id: str
 data_type: str
 payload_size: int
 qci: QCI
 timestamp: float
 arrival_order: int = field(default=0, compare=False)
 
 def __lt__(self, other):
 """Priority comparison - lower QCI number = higher priority"""
 if self.qci != other.qci:
 return self.qci < other.qci
 return self.arrival_order < other.arrival_order

class IoTQoSManager:
 """
 Manages IoT traffic with QoS prioritization
 Simulates how network operators handle different traffic classes
 """
 
 # QCI to latency SLA mapping (in milliseconds)
 QCI_SLA = {
 QCI.IOT_CRITICAL: 50, # 50ms - emergency services
 QCI.NON_GBR_MCIT: 100, # 100ms - critical data
 QCI.GBR_VIDEO: 150, # 150ms - video
 QCI.IOT_ASSET_TRACKING: 500, # 500ms - GPS tracking
 QCI.IOT_SMART_METER: 5000, # 5sec - delay tolerant
 QCI.IOT_ENVIRONMENTAL: 10000 # 10sec - very delay tolerant
 }
 
 def __init__(self, queue_size=1000):
 self.packet_queue = PriorityQueue(maxsize=queue_size)
 self.packets_processed = 0
 self.packets_dropped = 0
 self.wait_times = {qci: [] for qci in QCI}
 self.counter = 0 # For FIFO within same priority
 
 def enqueue(self, packet: QoSPacket) -> bool:
 """Add packet to queue with priority handling"""
 packet.arrival_order = self.counter
 self.counter += 1
 
 try:
 self.packet_queue.put(packet, block=False)
 return True
 except:
 self.packets_dropped += 1
 return False
 
 def dequeue(self) -> QoSPacket:
 """Remove highest priority packet from queue"""
 packet = self.packet_queue.get()
 self.packets_processed += 1
 return packet
 
 def get_sla_status(self, packet: QoSPacket) -> str:
 """Check if packet is within its SLA latency requirement"""
 current_time = time.time()
 wait_time = (current_time - packet.timestamp) * 1000 # Convert to ms
 sla = self.QCI_SLA.get(packet.qci, 10000)
 
 if wait_time <= sla:
 return "✓ WITHIN SLA"
 return "✗ SLA BREACH"

def simulate_iot_traffic():
 """Simulate different types of IoT traffic with varying QoS requirements"""
 
 print("=" * 70)
 print("IoT QoS Simulation - Network Operator Requirements")
 print("Quality of Service (QoS) and SLA Prioritization")
 print("=" * 70)
 
 qos_manager = IoTQoSManager()
 
 # Simulate various IoT devices transmitting data
 test_packets = [
 # Critical IoT - healthcare/emergency
 QoSPacket("device_001", "health_monitor", 256, QCI.IOT_CRITICAL, time.time()),
 QoSPacket("device_002", "fire_alarm", 64, QCI.IOT_CRITICAL, time.time()),
 
 # Asset tracking - moderate priority
 QoSPacket("device_003", "gps_tracker", 512, QCI.IOT_ASSET_TRACKING, time.time()),
 QoSPacket("device_004", "fleet_tracker", 512, QCI.IOT_ASSET_TRACKING, time.time()),
 
 # Smart metering - low priority, delay tolerant
 QoSPacket("device_005", "water_meter", 128, QCI.IOT_SMART_METER, time.time()),
 QoSPacket("device_006", "electricity_meter", 128, QCI.IOT_SMART_METER, time.time()),
 QoSPacket("device_007", "gas_meter", 128, QCI.IOT_SMART_METER, time.time()),
 
 # Environmental sensors - very low priority
 QoSPacket("device_008", "soil_moisture", 64, QCI.IOT_ENVIRONMENTAL, time.time()),
 QoSPacket("device_009", "temp_sensor", 64, QCI.IOT_ENVIRONMENTAL, time.time()),
 ]
 
 # Enqueue all packets
 print("\n[1] Enqueuing packets from various IoT devices...")
 for packet in test_packets:
 qos_manager.enqueue(packet)
 print(f" ✓ {packet.device_id}: {packet.data_type} (QCI={packet.qci})")
 
 # Process packets in priority order
 print("\n[2] Processing packets (higher priority first)...")
 print("-" * 70)
 print(f"{'Order':<6} {'Device':<12} {'Data Type':<18} {'QCI':<6} {'Priority':<15}")
 print("-" * 70)
 
 order = 1
 while not qos_manager.packet_queue.empty():
 packet = qos_manager.dequeue()
 priority_name = packet.qci.name
 print(f"{order:<6} {packet.device_id:<12} {packet.data_type:<18} {packet.qci:<6} {priority_name:<15}")
 order += 1
 
 print("-" * 70)
 
 # Summary
 print("\n[3] QoS Implementation Summary:")
 print("-" * 40)
 print("Network Operator Requirements Met:")
 print(" • Critical IoT (QCI 65) - Processed first")
 print(" • Asset Tracking (QCI 70) - Second priority") 
 print(" • Smart Meters (QCI 68) - Delay tolerant")
 print(" • Environmental (QCI 69) - Lowest priority")
 print("\nThis ensures:")
 print(" ✓ Emergency/alert data always gets through first")
 print(" ✓ Network congestion doesn't affect critical services")
 print(" ✓ Cost-effective handling of massive sensor networks")

if __name__ == "__main__":
 simulate_iot_traffic()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates the Quality of Service (QoS) requirements that network operators must implement. It uses a priority queue to process IoT packets based on their QoS Class Identifiers (QCIs), ensuring critical data like healthcare alerts or fire alarms are processed before routine sensor readings like smart meters. This directly addresses the QoS requirement from network operators, showing how tiered service levels work in practice.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) for enqueuing n packets, O(log n) for dequeuing

examples.title: C: IoT Network Scalability Simulation
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating network cell capacity and connection density management that network operators require for supporting massive IoT deployments
examples.explanation: This C implementation simulates network cell capacity management demonstrating the scalability requirements that network operators face with IoT deployments. It shows how traditional 4G cells are limited to around 2,000 connections while NB-IoT cells can support up to 100,000 connections through efficient resource management. The simulation demonstrates that when devices use PSM (Power Saving Mode), they consume significantly fewer network resources, which is a key requirement for operators deploying massive IoT networks.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for device registration, O(n) for device state lookup
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) where n is number of connected devices
